behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests whether the tool correctly detects and categorizes renames via git's rename detection mechanism. The failure would manifest only when running the tool on a real repository where git's `-M` flag (or equivalent rename detection) is critical. Parsing logic alone (Unit level) would miss failures in git command invocation, flag configuration, or threshold settings. A real repository with actual renamed files ensures the tool invokes git correctly and properly interprets rename markers in git output.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository with multiple commits. In one commit, rename a file (e.g., old.py → new.py), modify another file, and add a third file. Run the tool on this repository. Verify that: (1) the renamed file appears only in the renamed category with count 1, (2) it does not appear in deleted or added categories, (3) the modified and added files appear in their respective categories.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is a crash or unhandled error when the tool encounters binary file markers in git output (e.g., 'Binary files ... differ'). While parsing logic could be unit-tested with mocked git output, a real git repository with an actual binary file is necessary to generate authentic git output and ensure realistic edge cases are handled. Testing the tool's functions against actual git diff output from a real binary file is the minimum needed without requiring subprocess execution (System level).
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository and commit a binary file (e.g., an image file or compiled object). Modify the binary file in a subsequent commit. Run the tool on the repository (or run its parsing functions on real git diff output) and verify that: (1) the tool does not crash or raise an exception, (2) the binary file is counted in the appropriate category (modified), and (3) the output structure is valid and complete.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: The failure would be an error or exception when the tool processes a newly initialized repository with no commits. While this could theoretically be unit-tested by mocking git.log() as empty, a real empty git repository (created via git init without commits) is necessary to test actual git-level behavior when no commit history exists. Integration level (real empty repo, real git operations, function-level testing) is the minimum needed to catch failures in edge case handling.
behaviors.test_description: Create a new git repository via `git init` without making any commits. Run the tool on this repository (either as a subprocess or via function calls) and verify that: (1) the tool does not crash or raise an exception, (2) the output is a valid empty report (all counts zero or no entries), and (3) the output format matches that of reports from non-empty repositories (consistent structure).

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: This is the core correctness behavior. The failure (inaccurate counts) is only detectable by running the complete tool pipeline on a known repository state and comparing outputs to actual git history. The tool must be invoked as a subprocess reading a real repository and producing output that can be verified against the actual change counts from git commands. Partial testing (Unit with mocks, Integration at function level) cannot catch bugs in the complete execution path from git command invocation through output generation.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository with a known change scenario: commit exactly 2 files added, 1 deleted, 1 renamed, 3 modified (in sequence). Verify these counts independently using git commands (git diff --summary, git log --name-status). Invoke the tool as a subprocess on this repository and parse its output. Assert that reported counts match exactly: additions = 2, deletions = 1, renames = 1, modifications = 3.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires the tool to be executed in multiple different repository states (changes staged vs unstaged) and their outputs compared for consistency. The failure is only detectable by running the tool multiple times on the same repository with different staging configurations. Each execution must complete fully (subprocess invocation on real repository), and outputs must be compared. No lower level (mocks, function-level testing) can adequately test consistency across multiple repo state changes without executing the complete pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real git repository and make changes: modify 2 files, add 1 file, delete 1 file (in working directory). Stage all changes via `git add -A`. Run the tool as a subprocess and capture output (Output A). Unstage all changes via `git reset HEAD`. Run the tool again as a subprocess and capture output (Output B). Assert that Output A equals Output B (identical counts and categories in both outputs).
